question: What percentage of the world's population now lives in urban areas?
funFact: For the first time in human history, more than half the world's population moved to cities in 2007. By 2050, that figure is projected to reach 68%.

question: The demographic transition model describes a pattern where birth and death rates change as countries develop. What happens to both rates in the final stage?
wrongAnswers: Birth rate rises, death rate falls
Death rate rises, birth rate falls
funFact: Japan is a textbook example of late-stage demographic transition — very low birth and death rates, an aging population, and population decline without immigration.

question: A "primate city" in urban geography is best described as what?
correctAnswers: A city that is disproportionately large compared to the second city in the same country
wrongAnswers: The first city ever founded in a country
A city that dominates global finance
A capital city with political dominance
funFact: Bangkok is the world's most extreme primate city — it's roughly 40 times larger than Thailand's second city, Nonthaburi.

question: By 2050, roughly what proportion of the developed world is predicted to be urbanized?
funFact: While the developed world is projected to hit 86% urbanization by 2050, the developing world is also catching up fast — expected to reach 64%, up from under 50% today.

question: Which two continents are expected to account for the majority of urban population growth by 2050?
funFact: Africa and Asia together will absorb almost all of the projected 3 billion new urbanites by 2050, driven by rapid economic development and high birth rates.

question: The earliest cities in human history developed in which of the following civilizations?
funFact: The Indus Valley city of Mohenjo-daro featured grid-planned streets and sophisticated drainage systems as early as 2500 BCE — urban planning that wouldn't reappear in Europe for millennia.

question: What key distinction separates "urbanization" from "urban growth" in human geography?
correctAnswers: Urbanization is the proportion of national population living in cities; urban growth is the absolute increase in urban population
wrongAnswers: Urbanization refers to new city construction; urban growth refers to population increases
Urbanization applies only to developed nations; urban growth applies to developing ones
Urban growth is the proportion living in cities; urbanization is the absolute number
They are interchangeable terms describing the same phenomenon
Urbanization measures density; urban growth measures city area
funFact: A country can have high urban growth (millions more people in cities each year) while its urbanization rate stays flat — if the rural population is growing just as fast.

question: Which two revolutions in 18th-century Britain broke the long-standing equilibrium between rural and urban populations?
funFact: Before these twin revolutions, cities had never been able to grow large because food production could not keep pace. The Agricultural Revolution freed up rural labour; the Industrial Revolution gave it urban jobs.

question: In England and Wales, the proportion of people living in cities of more than 20,000 jumped from 17% in 1801 to what figure by 1891?
funFact: This 37-percentage-point leap in just 90 years was among the fastest urbanization rates ever recorded, driven by factory work pulling millions from the countryside into industrial cities like Manchester and Birmingham.

question: Greater Seoul is home to approximately what share of South Korea's total national population?
funFact: Greater Seoul's dominance is extraordinary by global standards — half a country squeezed into one metro area. By comparison, Greater London holds only about 13% of the UK's population.

question: Although Manila is the capital of the Philippines, which city is actually the largest municipality in Greater Manila by population?
funFact: Quezon City has 2.7 million residents compared to Manila's 1.6 million — a counterintuitive fact since Manila gives its name to the whole metropolitan area and remains the national capital.

question: The 'wealthy west end, poor east end' divide seen in many large European cities is most commonly attributed to which environmental factor?
correctAnswers: Prevailing south-west winds carrying coal smoke eastward
wrongAnswers: Rivers flowing west to east, dumping waste downstream
Eastern districts receiving less sunlight due to city orientation
Nobility preferring western land as a religious tradition
Eastern soils being less suitable for construction
Trade routes entering cities from the west, raising land values there
funFact: In Victorian London, the east end became synonymous with poverty and smog while the west end housed the elite — the same south-westerly wind pattern can be observed shaping city layouts across Paris, Berlin, and many other European capitals.

question: Historian Eric Hobsbawm described 19th-century urban development as "a gigantic process of class segregation" in which 1962 book?
wrongAnswers: The Making of the English Working Class
funFact: Hobsbawm's observation captures how industrialisation physically sorted the classes: factory owners built mansions upwind while workers crowded into smoky slums nearby — a spatial inequality that shaped cities for generations.
